old_claim.note: Learned and scientific societies did not enable the earliest printed compendia, but their journals, proceedings, and specialist contributor networks later scaled arts-and-sciences reference works.

new_claim_absent: No scientific-society edge remains because the node is locked to Reisch's 1503 printed compendium, which predates the documented 1560 scientific-society category.

source_shape.source_locator: The Freiburg library catalog dates the printed Margarita philosophica edition to 1503 and exposes its twelve-book contents.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The scoped compendium was already printed and organized decades before the first directly documented scientific society in this graph.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Later academies may have supported other reference works, but that scaling history falls outside a node explicitly locked to the 1503 edition.

ontology_before: Later collaborative encyclopedias were blended into an early single-author printed compendium through a scaling edge.
ontology_after: The Margarita node represents its own 1503 production context without importing later academy networks.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: encyclopedic_compendiums_knowledge_organization no longer directly depends on scientific_societies.
would_reject_if: Evidence showed a scientific society operating before 1503 directly sponsored, compiled, printed, or distributed Reisch's Margarita philosophica.
short_reason: The 1503 compendium predates the evidence-locked scientific-society category.
